questions.question: What is the Separating Axis Theorem (SAT)?
options: Two convex polyhedra don't overlap iff there exists an axis where their projections don't overlap. Test candidate axes.
questions.correctAnswer: Two convex polyhedra don't overlap iff there exists an axis where their projections don't overlap. Test candidate axes.
questions.explanation: SAT: project both shapes onto axis. If projections don't overlap → separating axis found → NO collision. If ALL candidate axes overlap → collision.

questions.question: Which axes must be tested for OBB-OBB SAT?
questions.correctAnswer: 15 axes: 3 face normals of A + 3 face normals of B + 9 cross products of edges (3×3)
acceptableAnswers: face normals plus edge cross products
questions.explanation: Box A: 3 face normals. Box B: 3 face normals. Cross products: 3 edges of A × 3 edges of B = 9 axes. Total: 15. Test all 15.

questions.e: SAT is exact for convex polyhedra. Number of axes tested depends on shape complexity. OBB-OBB = 15 axes. Triangle-triangle = 11 axes.
questions.explanation: OBB-OBB tests 15 axes: 3 face normals of A + 3 face normals of B + 9 cross products (each edge of A × each edge of B). The cross product axes catch cases where neither face normal separates — two boxes nearly touching at corners.
questions.question: What shape pairs can SAT handle?
options: Any convex polyhedra — works for OBB, convex hulls, capsules. Requires enumerating candidate separating axes.
questions.correctAnswer: Any convex polyhedra — works for OBB, convex hulls, capsules. Requires enumerating candidate separating axes.

questions.e: SAT: fixed axis set, O(num_faces²) for OBB. GJK: iterative, ~3-6 iterations for convex shapes. GJK is more general.
questions.explanation: SAT is exact for convex polyhedra — you enumerate a finite set of candidate axes, and if ANY separates, there is no collision. For general convex shapes, SAT works but the axis set depends on the shape complexity.
questions.question: What is the difference between SAT and GJK?
options: SAT tests static set of axes (good for boxes). GJK iteratively finds separating axis (works for ANY convex shape, no pre-computed axes).
questions.correctAnswer: SAT tests static set of axes (good for boxes). GJK iteratively finds separating axis (works for ANY convex shape, no pre-computed axes).

questions.e: Edge-edge cross products catch cases where neither face normal separates. Example: two boxes almost touching at corners, no face normal separates.
questions.explanation: Project both shapes onto the candidate axis: interval = [min(vertices·axis), max(vertices·axis)]. If the two intervals don't overlap, this axis separates the shapes → NO collision. If ALL candidate axes have overlapping intervals → collision.
questions.question: Why test cross products of edges for OBB SAT?
options: Two boxes can have a separating axis that is perpendicular to an edge from each — the cross product direction. 3×3=9 additional axes.
questions.correctAnswer: Two boxes can have a separating axis that is perpendicular to an edge from each — the cross product direction. 3×3=9 additional axes.

questions.question: What does GJK compute?
options: Whether two convex shapes overlap — by checking if the origin is inside their Minkowski difference
questions.correctAnswer: Whether two convex shapes overlap — by checking if the origin is inside their Minkowski difference
questions.explanation: GJK: iteratively builds simplex in Minkowski difference space (A - B). If origin is inside simplex → overlap. Support function finds extreme points.

questions.question: What is the Minkowski difference used in GJK?
options: A - B = {a - b | a∈A, b∈B}. If origin ∈ (A - B), then A and B intersect.
questions.correctAnswer: A - B = {a - b | a∈A, b∈B}. If origin ∈ (A - B), then A and B intersect.
questions.explanation: Minkowski difference transforms intersection test into origin-inclusion test. GJK doesn't need to explicitly compute A - B — uses support function.

questions.question: What is the support function in GJK?
options: Returns the point in a shape furthest in a given direction — used to build the simplex iteratively
questions.correctAnswer: Returns the point in a shape furthest in a given direction — used to build the simplex iteratively
questions.explanation: support(shape, direction) = argmax(p·direction for p in shape). GJK calls this to find next simplex vertex. O(1) for simple shapes, O(n) for meshes.

questions.e: GJK detects overlap. EPA computes MTV (Minimum Translation Vector) — how far and in what direction to push shapes apart. GJK+EPA = full collision response.
questions.explanation: EPA (Expanding Polytope Algorithm) runs after GJK detects overlap. It expands the simplex outward until it touches the Minkowski difference boundary, computing the Minimum Translation Vector — the shortest distance and direction to separate the shapes.
questions.question: What is EPA (Expanding Polytope Algorithm)?
options: Extends GJK — after detecting overlap, EPA expands simplex to find penetration depth and contact normal
questions.correctAnswer: Extends GJK — after detecting overlap, EPA expands simplex to find penetration depth and contact normal

questions.e: Minkowski difference shrinks A to a point and expands B by A's shape. If that expanded shape contains the origin, they overlap.
questions.explanation: A - B = {a-b | a∈A, b∈B}. This transforms the intersection test into an origin-inclusion test: if the origin is inside A-B, then A and B share at least one point (intersect). GJK tests origin inclusion without explicitly computing A-B.
questions.question: What is the Minkowski difference?
options: A - B = {a-b | a∈A, b∈B}. If origin ∈ (A-B), A and B intersect. Transforms intersection to origin test.
questions.correctAnswer: A - B = {a-b | a∈A, b∈B}. If origin ∈ (A-B), A and B intersect. Transforms intersection to origin test.

questions.e: support(shape, d) = extreme point in direction d. O(1) for sphere/box. O(n) for convex hull. GJK never needs explicit Minkowski difference.
questions.explanation: The support function returns the point in a shape furthest in a given direction: argmax(p·d for p in shape). For a sphere: center + radius·d̂. For a box: corner with signs matching direction. GJK uses support functions to build the simplex iteratively.
questions.question: What is the support function in GJK?
options: Returns point in shape furthest in given direction: argmax(p·d for p in shape). GJK calls this to build simplex.
questions.correctAnswer: Returns point in shape furthest in given direction: argmax(p·d for p in shape). GJK calls this to build simplex.

questions.question: What does Newton's Second Law (F=ma) govern in physics engines?
options: Linear acceleration: a = F/m. Integrate acceleration to get velocity, velocity to get position.
questions.correctAnswer: Linear acceleration: a = F/m. Integrate acceleration to get velocity, velocity to get position.
questions.explanation: Forces cause acceleration. Gravity: F=mg. Friction, springs, constraints all produce forces. Integration accumulates force effects over time.

questions.question: What is the inertia tensor?
options: 3×3 matrix describing how mass is distributed — determines angular acceleration from torque: τ = I·α
questions.correctAnswer: 3×3 matrix describing how mass is distributed — determines angular acceleration from torque: τ = I·α
questions.explanation: Rotational analog of mass. Inertia tensor depends on shape and mass distribution. Diagonalized in principal axes. Harder to rotate around axes with larger inertia.

questions.e: ω is a 3D vector. Direction = axis of rotation (right-hand rule). magnitude = radians per second. L = I·ω (angular momentum).
questions.explanation: The inertia tensor I is a 3×3 matrix describing mass distribution. The same torque applied around different axes produces different angular acceleration depending on how mass is distributed. τ = I·α (rotational Newton's Second Law).
questions.question: What is angular velocity?
options: ω — rate of rotation around an axis. Direction = rotation axis, magnitude = speed (rad/s).
questions.correctAnswer: ω — rate of rotation around an axis. Direction = rotation axis, magnitude = speed (rad/s).

questions.e: Rotational mass. Sphere: diagonal (all equal). Box: diagonal (different per axis). Non-diagonal for arbitrary shapes. Diagonalized in body frame.
questions.explanation: 6 DOF: position(3) + orientation(3). Orientation can be Euler angles (3), quaternion (4 with 1 constraint), or rotation matrix (9 with 6 constraints). State vector includes: position, velocity, orientation, angular velocity.
questions.question: What is the inertia tensor?
options: 3×3 matrix describing mass distribution. τ = I·α (torque = inertia tensor × angular acceleration). Depends on shape.
questions.correctAnswer: 3×3 matrix describing mass distribution. τ = I·α (torque = inertia tensor × angular acceleration). Depends on shape.
3 translational (x, y, z) + 3 rotational (pitch, yaw, roll)
acceptableAnswers: x y z pitch yaw roll
questions.explanation: 6 DOF = complete description of position and orientation in 3D space. A rigid body's state = position(3) + velocity(3) + orientation(4) + angularVelocity(3).
questions.question: What are the six degrees of freedom of a rigid body?

questions.e: COM = weighted average of mass distribution. Linear dynamics use COM position and velocity. Rotation is around COM.
questions.explanation: Center of mass is the weighted average of mass distribution. Linear dynamics (F=ma) apply at COM. Forces applied away from COM create torque (τ = r × F) and cause rotation. COM simplifies: translate like a point mass, rotate around COM.
questions.question: What is the center of mass?
options: The point where all mass is concentrated for linear motion. Forces applied off-center create torque (rotation).
questions.correctAnswer: The point where all mass is concentrated for linear motion. Forces applied off-center create torque (rotation).

questions.question: What is Euler integration?
options: Simplest: pos += vel·dt; vel += acc·dt. O(dt) error per step. Unstable for stiff systems.
questions.correctAnswer: Simplest: pos += vel·dt; vel += acc·dt. O(dt) error per step. Unstable for stiff systems.
questions.explanation: Explicit Euler: first-order. Cheap but diverges for oscillatory systems unless dt is very small. Game physics at 60Hz often uses semi-implicit Euler.

questions.question: What is semi-implicit Euler (symplectic)?
options: vel += acc·dt FIRST, then pos += vel·dt. More stable than explicit Euler for same cost. Standard in game physics.
questions.correctAnswer: vel += acc·dt FIRST, then pos += vel·dt. More stable than explicit Euler for same cost. Standard in game physics.
questions.explanation: Symplectic Euler preserves energy better than explicit. Order matters: update velocity first, then use NEW velocity for position. UE physics uses this.

questions.e: Verlet: no explicit velocity. Velocity = (pos(t+dt)-pos(t-dt))/(2·dt). Used in molecular dynamics, cloth simulation.
questions.explanation: Explicit Euler: v(t+dt) = v(t) + a(t)·dt using acceleration from START of step. The error accumulates, adding energy to the system — orbits spiral outward, springs oscillate with growing amplitude. Semi-implicit uses v(t+dt) for position update, improving stability.
questions.question: What is Verlet integration?
options: pos(t+dt)=2·pos(t)-pos(t-dt)+acc·dt². Velocity-free, time-reversible, good energy conservation.
questions.correctAnswer: pos(t+dt)=2·pos(t)-pos(t-dt)+acc·dt². Velocity-free, time-reversible, good energy conservation.

questions.e: Fixed timestep = stability, reproducibility. Variable = frame-synced. UE physics runs at fixed rate (default 60Hz) with accumulator for sub-steps.
questions.explanation: RK4 evaluates the derivative at 4 points within a step and takes a weighted average. This gives O(dt⁵) error per step — much more accurate than Euler's O(dt²). Used where accuracy matters more than speed (cinematic physics, scientific simulation).
questions.question: What is fixed vs variable timestep in physics?
options: Fixed: deterministic, reproducible. Variable: frame-rate matched. UE: fixed physics timestep with sub-stepping for stability.
questions.correctAnswer: Fixed: deterministic, reproducible. Variable: frame-rate matched. UE: fixed physics timestep with sub-stepping for stability.

questions.e: Explicit Euler evaluates derivative at START of step. Semi-implicit uses updated velocity for position — much better energy behavior.
questions.explanation: F = -k·x - c·v. Spring force proportional to displacement (-k·x, Hooke's law). Damping force proportional to velocity (-c·v). Stiff springs (large k) require tiny dt for explicit methods to remain stable.
questions.question: Why does explicit Euler gain energy?
options: Each step slightly overshoots the true trajectory — error accumulates, orbits expand, springs explode. Semi-implicit fixes this.
questions.correctAnswer: Each step slightly overshoots the true trajectory — error accumulates, orbits expand, springs explode. Semi-implicit fixes this.

questions.e: RK4: 4 evaluations per step. k1=f(t,y), k2=f(t+dt/2,y+k1·dt/2), k3, k4. Average with weights [1,2,2,1]. Used where accuracy > speed.
questions.explanation: Verlet: x(t+dt) = 2x(t) - x(t-dt) + a(t)·dt². No explicit velocity storage. Velocity derived as (x(t+dt)-x(t-dt))/(2dt). Time-reversible and excellent energy conservation — standard for molecular dynamics and cloth simulation.
questions.question: What is RK4 integration?
options: 4th-order Runge-Kutta: evaluates derivative at 4 points per step, weighted average. O(dt⁵) error. Expensive but very accurate.
questions.correctAnswer: 4th-order Runge-Kutta: evaluates derivative at 4 points per step, weighted average. O(dt⁵) error. Expensive but very accurate.

questions.e: Hooke's law + damping. k=stiffness, c=damping. Stiff springs (large k) require tiny dt for explicit methods. Implicit methods handle stiff springs better.
questions.explanation: Fixed timestep = deterministic, reproducible physics. Variable = frame-rate matched but non-deterministic. UE runs physics at a fixed rate (default 60Hz) using an accumulator — renders at variable FPS, steps physics at fixed intervals with sub-stepping.
questions.question: What is the spring-mass-damper system?
options: F = -k·x - c·v. Spring force proportional to displacement, damping proportional to velocity. Stiff springs need small dt.
questions.correctAnswer: F = -k·x - c·v. Spring force proportional to displacement, damping proportional to velocity. Stiff springs need small dt.

questions.question: What is a constraint in physics simulation?
options: A rule enforcing a relationship between bodies — distance joint, hinge, contact (non-penetration). Solved iteratively.
questions.correctAnswer: A rule enforcing a relationship between bodies — distance joint, hinge, contact (non-penetration). Solved iteratively.
questions.explanation: Constraints: equality (joints) and inequality (contacts). Solved via sequential impulse (projected Gauss-Seidel) in most game engines.

questions.e: Projected Gauss-Seidel. Solve each constraint independently, repeat. Converges over iterations. UE's PhysX/Chaos use 4-8 iterations per frame.
questions.explanation: A Lagrange multiplier λ is the force magnitude needed to enforce a constraint. Each constraint equation C(x)=0 produces one scalar λ. The solver finds the λ values that satisfy all constraints simultaneously via the linear system J·v + b = 0.
questions.question: What is sequential impulse solving?
options: Iteratively resolves constraints one at a time — projects velocities to satisfy constraint, repeats. Standard in game physics.
questions.correctAnswer: Iteratively resolves constraints one at a time — projects velocities to satisfy constraint, repeats. Standard in game physics.

questions.e: λ = constraint force. J·v + b = 0 is the velocity-level constraint. Solver computes λ that satisfies all constraints.
questions.explanation: Baumgarte stabilization adds a position correction term: b = (β/dt)·C(x) where β≈0.1-0.3. This proportional feedback drives position errors toward zero over multiple timesteps. Too high β causes jitter; too low allows visible penetration.
questions.question: What is a Lagrange multiplier in constraint solving?
options: The force magnitude needed to enforce a constraint. Each constraint equation produces one λ. Solver finds all λ simultaneously.
questions.correctAnswer: The force magnitude needed to enforce a constraint. Each constraint equation produces one λ. Solver finds all λ simultaneously.

questions.e: Baumgarte: b = (β/dt)·C(x). β = 0.1-0.3. Corrects position errors gradually. Too high = jitter; too low = visible penetration.
questions.explanation: C(x) = |p₁-p₂| - L = 0. The Jacobian J is the direction vector between the two points. When distance = rest length L, constraint is satisfied. When |p₁-p₂| > L, constraint is violated and solver applies corrective impulses along the connection direction.
questions.question: What is Baumgarte stabilization?
options: Adds position error correction to velocity constraint: bias term proportional to penetration depth. Prevents drift.
questions.correctAnswer: Adds position error correction to velocity constraint: bias term proportional to penetration depth. Prevents drift.

questions.e: C(x) = |p1-p2| - L. Jacobian J = direction vector. Satisfied when distance = rest length. Violation = stretch/compression.
questions.explanation: Sequential impulse (Projected Gauss-Seidel) solves constraints one at a time: compute impulse for constraint i, apply velocity update, move to constraint i+1. Repeated iterations converge to the global solution. PhysX uses ~4-10 iterations.
questions.question: What is a distance constraint?
options: |p1 - p2| = restLength. Maintains fixed distance between two points. Used for ropes, ragdoll joints.
questions.correctAnswer: |p1 - p2| = restLength. Maintains fixed distance between two points. Used for ropes, ragdoll joints.

questions.question: What is Time of Impact (TOI)?
options: The exact time two moving shapes first touch. CCD computes TOI to prevent tunneling for fast-moving objects.
questions.correctAnswer: The exact time two moving shapes first touch. CCD computes TOI to prevent tunneling for fast-moving objects.
questions.explanation: TOI = t ∈ [0,1] where first contact occurs. Conservative advancement: iterative search for TOI by advancing shapes until distance < epsilon.

questions.e: Conservative advancement: move by distance to closest feature (safe step). Compute new closest distance. Repeat until distance < epsilon. Bullet, PhysX use this.
questions.explanation: TOI ∈ [0,1] is the exact time within the timestep when two moving shapes first contact. CCD sweeps shapes along their trajectories to compute TOI. The result positions objects exactly at the point of contact, preventing tunneling.
questions.question: What is conservative advancement for TOI?
options: Iterative search: advance shapes by safe distance (guaranteed no collision), check again. Converges to TOI.
questions.correctAnswer: Iterative search: advance shapes by safe distance (guaranteed no collision), check again. Converges to TOI.

questions.e: TOI ∈ [0,1]. Conservative advancement: iteratively shrink time interval until contact. TOI=0.5 means collision at midpoint of timestep.
questions.explanation: Conservative advancement iteratively narrows the TOI interval: compute closest distance d between shapes, advance time by d/(relative speed) — guaranteed no collision in this sub-interval. Repeat until distance < epsilon. Converges to exact TOI.
questions.question: What is time of impact (TOI)?
options: The exact time within a timestep when two moving shapes first touch. CCD computes TOI to position objects at contact.
questions.correctAnswer: The exact time within a timestep when two moving shapes first touch. CCD computes TOI to position objects at contact.

questions.e: CA: advance shapes by d/(max speed). Recompute distance. Repeat until d < epsilon. Converges to TOI. Used by PhysX, Bullet.
questions.explanation: CCD sweeps actual shape geometry precisely (expensive, accurate). Speculative contacts expand contact tolerance by velocity·dt — effectively inflating AABBs to catch fast objects. Much cheaper but can produce false positives. UE5 Chaos uses speculative contacts by default.
questions.question: What is conservative advancement?
options: Iterative TOI search: compute closest distance d, advance by d/(relative speed) — guaranteed no collision this step, repeat.
questions.correctAnswer: Iterative TOI search: compute closest distance d, advance by d/(relative speed) — guaranteed no collision this step, repeat.

questions.e: UE5 Chaos: speculative contacts = inflated AABB by velocity*dt. Cheaper than CCD. Good for most fast objects. CCD for critical cases (bullets).
questions.explanation: Bullet-through-paper: a bullet at 1000 m/s travels 41m between 24fps frames. Discrete checks at frame boundaries miss the 1cm wall entirely. CCD sweeps the bullet's path as a continuous volume and detects the intermediate collision.
questions.question: What is the difference between CCD and speculative contacts?
options: CCD: sweeps shapes precisely (expensive). Speculative: expands contact threshold by velocity, catches fast objects via inflated AABB (cheaper).
questions.correctAnswer: CCD: sweeps shapes precisely (expensive). Speculative: expands contact threshold by velocity, catches fast objects via inflated AABB (cheaper).
